TextENGNew: Warm.
I wonder why. Simply gazing at her fills me with a gentle, spreading warmth from somewhere deep in my chest.
A comfortable feeling.
Why is that, I wonder. Even though I cannot reach out and touch her... merely being near her brings a strange, inexplicable peace to my heart.
My body drifts and floats beside that child—and so, too, does my heart.
She knows nothing.
She understands nothing.
Out of a mind and memory as blank and white as freshly fallen snow... the child speaks words she has only just learned.
But her tongue is too clumsy for them; what reaches me is something so far removed from the words' true meaning as to be unrecognizable.
She tries again in a fluster, but the result is the same. And so she falls silent, the faintest shadow of disappointment passing over her face.
Seeing that, I draw gently closer and lean my ear toward her lips.
I cannot make out what she is trying to tell me. ...Or perhaps there is no meaning in her voice at all.
Even so, when I hear it, my heart quickens—a warmth seeps through me from within, spreading to every corner of my body... and I cannot help but feel a deep, aching nostalgia.
Yes... it must be something very dear, something very beloved, that I left behind long ago.
And as proof of that, without even realizing it, I stretch both hands out toward the source of that voice and call to it with everything in me.
I don't want to let go.
I want to be with her forever.
May this wish somehow reach you, wherever you are.
And yet.
...Who could have foreseen that that longing would become the beginning of a long, heavy, and painful journey for that child?
Not even a god could have.
Because I—
When I opened my eyes, I found myself lying in a futon.
The faint warmth of sunlight shimmered hazily beyond the window.
...Morning already.
The distant chatter of birds drifted in faintly from outside the window.
Mingled among them, the cicadas' voices rang like the patter of passing rain.
The comforter felt strangely heavy. A very large futon, at that... Did I have something like this?
No—that wasn't it.
The ceiling was high. No water stains from rain, either; the wood grain traced its rippled patterns clear and distinct.

An unfamiliar room. —No, that wasn't quite right. This ceiling did still live somewhere in the margins of my memory. I had simply forgotten, for it had been so long since I last saw it. ...Or rather, it was taking time to surface.
...Come to think of it—when was it that I used to find that pattern unsettling?
As my consciousness gradually settled into itself, from somewhere came the faint scent of incense.
...That, too, felt strangely familiar.
"Haah..."
Still half-drowsed with sleep, I lifted myself from the futon. A white towel fell away before my eyes, and all at once my forehead blazed with heat.
A heavy, dull ache. A numb, leaden weariness spread from the nape of my neck to the back of my head.
I picked up the towel where it had draped itself over the comforter. ...It was cold. That coldness was what finally made me understand why I had been sleeping here.
"Right, this place..."
My home.
The Furude home—which I had not returned to in so long, not since I began living together with Satoko.
And this had been my room.
Somehow... I knew. This must be the world inside a dream—the world that appears when the gears of time are wound back...
"...How nostalgic."
It was rare for me to fall into this kind of sentimentality. After living through so long a span, and dreaming so many dreams... how long had it been since I last thought about this room?
That desk.
That clock.
The clumsy portrait of my mother I worked so hard to draw back in the little ones' class.
I had passed them every day and never given them a thought, but now... they were all so dear to me. The strange sensation rising slowly from within my chest was not unpleasant in the least.
Something at the edge of my vision caught my attention, and I crawled out from the futon.
A wave of dizziness washed over me as I stood, and my feet were unsteady as I crossed toward it... but I reached down and picked it up.
"Long time no see... have you been well?"
I cradled it gently and pressed a soft kiss to the tip of its nose.
It was nothing remarkable—only a stuffed bear. But it was precious to me, for my mother had given it to me. After my mother disappeared, it had vanished as well, for reasons I never understood.
When I spotted a bear displayed as a shooting-gallery prize at a festival stall, I had for just a moment almost believed it had come back to me. ...Of course that was impossible.
"That's right..."
Something clicked into place, and I turned my ear toward the sounds drifting through the shoji from the kitchen.
A tidy, rhythmic sound—a knife working its way through vegetables.
Beneath it, I could faintly hear something simmering in a pot... rice porridge, most likely, being made for me.
And there—cast faintly in silhouette against the shoji, half-lit by the light behind it—was a figure's back...
Even just a shadow... there was no way I could mistake it.
My chest drew tight with a sweet, unbearable ache.
Nostalgia and something fierce and burning overflowed without end, and the back of my throat... trembled.
That figure I had been trying to forget.
Someone whose memory was painful to return to—who had filled me with sadness and anger... and whom I had loved so dearly.
All of that was there, just beyond this shoji.
I wanted to open this door.
I wanted to fling it open and throw myself upon her back.
I wanted to see her face turn—startled, then gently reproving—and look back at me with a soft smile, close enough to touch.
...And yet.
I couldn't.
If I opened the shoji, this dream would surely end.
...No, that wasn't it.
Perhaps my mother would no longer smile for me. That fear and dread rooted my feet and would not let me move.
"The reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama."
As the villagers celebrated me and the village elders came to revere and fear me—the distance between my mother and me had grown, and grown, and grown.
I only wanted to be accepted.
By my mother, more than anyone.
To have her understand about my friend who was always beside me—"Hanyuu"—
That the reason I was better at cooking than anyone was because of her.
That the reason I could know what would happen next was because of her.
...I had no wish to show off or make a display of being special.
It was my friend who had shown me all of that.
The reason I could do things no one had taught me—not my mother, not anyone—was that she truly was beside me.
And so.
At every opportunity I boasted, wanting her to believe what I was saying... but my mother was not pleased.
On the contrary—it had unnerved her, and made her distrust me.
At first I was sad. So I tried my hardest to make her understand.
But when I realized it was no use, I grew angry.
Why.
Why.
You are my parent. My mother.
...I had believed you were the one person who had to understand me, even if no one else in the world did.
No matter how unbelievable it sounded, you were supposed to know it was not a lie, and offer me the comfort of being believed.
...And yet... you...
"...What a fool... it wasn't like that at all..."
Looking back at that past self, I felt a sadness that was almost funny.

No—it was not only my mother's fault. For all that her words were harsh and her assumptions unyielding, there was a self-righteous edge to her, but she worried about me in her own way, and she tried her hardest.

If I had simply said nothing to begin with, it could have ended there. If I had accepted things as natural, rejoiced in the way a child my age would, thrown my tantrums... surely my mother would have been as tender with me as she was in those earliest days.
Even Hanyuu—if I had taken my time and explained her slowly, carefully... I can believe now that my mother, given enough time, would surely have come to understand.
And yet.
It was I who failed to do that. It was none other than I who gave up on my own.
I sulked, I turned cold... and on the other hand, I looked down on her and made a show of myself.
—What feelings that must have stirred in her... by the time I thought of it, it was already too late.
Even with Hanyuu's power, I could not return to a time far enough back to do it over.
Lonely.
Even now... it is just so terribly lonely.
But...
Even so...
"Mom—!"
Even just one word of apology would be enough. I had to tell her—I had to, no matter what.
I'm sorry for being glad when it rained.
I'm sorry for not appreciating the lunches you made.
I'm sorry for not returning your love.
And—and—
With that feeling pressing up through me, I... slid the shoji open—
I woke.
Somewhere in the distance, a cricket was singing.
This was the outbuilding of the Kimiyoshi house—the place I had long since come to call home.
In the futon laid out right beside mine, Satoko's profile was peaceful with sleep.
Through the window, the bright disc of the moon cast its pale, diffuse light over the darkened room.
"...Oh, that's right..."
I dragged myself up from sleep-dulled consciousness and noisy, tangled memory. ...I remembered: I had rewound time, and had returned once more to that moment.
Yes... the tragedy that would visit Hinamizawa.
The happy, lively, quietly fulfilled and unremarkable days I had spent with my friends leading up to it—repeated over and over.
And there was one thing that had been different this time...
"—Miyo Takano..."
This time alone, I had remembered clearly.
The true culprit who had slashed open my belly and killed me.
She had exploited "the curse of Oyashiro-sama" to trample upon the hopes and efforts of my friends... scheming to fulfill her wretched plan within this small and sealed world. Our enemy.
For a time, I had even relied on her, just a little.
I had thought she was a kind person who cared about Satoko and me.
But—
But all of that... I would forget.
As long as that woman existed, I... and we could never be happy.
This time... this time, I would stop her, without fail.
This mad repetition of the world...
My head had finally cooled.
It had been a very long time since I had let myself feel so emotional.
I suppose it was because last time... I had witnessed what happened to Keiichi and the others.
"—Right."
I raised myself from the futon and called out to the companion who was always at my side.
"...Hanyuu, what time is it now? What year of Showa, and what month and day?"
The question I asked first, every single time.
How much time remained before the day of fate that had claimed me, without fail, through every world until now.
That span had been growing steadily shorter; by now there was precious little time left to spend on preparations.
But... this time was a little different.
I knew who was an ally—and who was an enemy.
That alone was a precious, precious factor I had never once had in any of the worlds before.
I did not yet know how to fight.
Whether I could win—whether I could escape the shackles of this fate—I still did not know.
"...Even so."
Even so, I felt as though I could see the faintest glimmer of light.
It was the light at the end of a long, long, pitch-dark tunnel—so small it seemed on the verge of being swallowed by the surrounding darkness... and yet so strong.
So I could still fight.
I could step forward on the feet I had risen to, and gather my strength into the fist I had raised.
And for that—how much time did I have left?
"...Hanyuu?"
Something was wrong.
No voice. No sound.
No—more than that... no presence at all.
She was always chattering away at me so relentlessly that she sometimes made me uncomfortable.
"Hanyuu? What's wrong, Hanyuu?"
"Hanyuu...?"
This had never happened before.
"Hanyuu?"
Hanyuu was not answering.
"Hanyuu?!"
Hanyuu... wasn't there?!
"Hanyuu—?!"
"...Nngh, what's wrong, Rika...?"
"I... meep. It's nothing."
I forced out a laugh with my usual speech habits, awkward as it was. ...This was bad. I was so shaken that I couldn't manage the switch.
I had shouted without thinking... forgetting that Satoko was sleeping right beside me.
"Making strange noises in the middle of the night... Are you feeling all right?"

"M... meep. It's nothing.
I think I must have had a bad dream."
I forced out a laugh with my usual speech habits, awkward as it was. ...This was bad. I was so shaken that I couldn't manage the switch.

"Is that so... haah...
Then go back to sleep..."

"...Mii... that's what I'll do.
Good night, Satoko."
"Good night..."
With that, Satoko—who must have been half-asleep already—closed her eyes, and before long her quiet breathing resumed. Once I was certain of that, I slipped from my bed and stepped outside.
"Hanyuu? Can't you hear me, Hanyuu? Answer me!"
I called desperately into the empty air.
She should have been here. She was always here.
Wherever I went, whatever I did, whatever I thought... she always knew it immediately.
And yet this time—why?!
"...Hanyuu..."
I stood there in a daze, staring up at the sky.
The moon hung above the night sky, casting its light down over me where I stood in the dark.
The cold of my sleep-dampened skin sent a shiver through me—a trembling as if even my blood might freeze.
A world in which, at last, hope had seemed to glimmer.
The enemy was visible. So was her purpose. ...The outcome remained uncertain.
And I had finally been able to believe in how extraordinary my friends truly were.
I was not alone.
Rena was there. Mion and Shion were there. Satoko was there. ...And Keiichi was there.
If we united our strength—this time, finally, I had believed I was allowed to hope. A last chance, perhaps the very last.
And yet... and yet...
"...Hanyuu—!"
Struck dumb with the weight of what it meant... I crumpled where I stood, sinking to my knees.
The one who had been the least reliable... and yet the one who had given my heart the most peace...
Was nowhere to be found in this world.
